content: Use short present simple sentences: My name is..., I am from..., I live in.... Keep sentences to 5–8 words.
exercise: Introduce yourself in 4 short sentences (name, age, country, hobby).

content: Practise cardinal numbers (1–100), ordinal numbers (first, second), and date formats: the 5th of June / June 5th.
exercise: Write your birthday, 3 important dates in your life, and count to 20 in sentences.

content: Use present simple for habits. Add time expressions: in the morning, at noon, at night. Verbs: wake up, eat, go, come home.
exercise: Describe your daily routine in 5 sentences using time expressions.

content: For regular verbs, add -ed in simple past. Many common verbs are irregular (go→went, eat→ate). Use time markers: yesterday, last week, in 2020.
exercise: Write 6 sentences about what you did yesterday, using both regular and irregular verbs.

content: Use connectors to join ideas: and (addition), but (contrast), because (reason), so (result), then / after that (sequence).
exercise: Combine 8 short ideas into 4 longer connected sentences using different connectors.

content: Form questions with do/does (present), did (past), and wh-words (what, where, when, why, who, how). Watch subject-verb inversion.
exercise: Write 8 questions you would ask a new classmate — use at least 4 different wh-words.

content: Use 'going to' for plans and intentions. Use 'will' for spontaneous decisions and predictions. Don't mix them randomly.
exercise: Write 3 sentences with 'going to' (your plans) and 3 sentences with 'will' (predictions about the world).

content: Use opinion frames: I think..., In my opinion..., As far as I'm concerned.... Support each opinion with one reason or example.
exercise: Write your opinion on social media (6–8 sentences). Use at least 3 different opinion frames.

content: Use comparatives (more/less + adj, -er), superlatives (most/least, -est), and contrast phrases: while, whereas, on the other hand.
exercise: Compare city life and rural life in 8 sentences. Use at least two contrast phrases.

content: Set the scene in the opening (time, place, character). Use past simple for main events, past continuous for background. Include one turning point.
exercise: Write a short story (10–12 sentences) about an unexpected event. Include background, a turning point, and a resolution.

content: Use modals for ability (can/could), obligation (must/have to/should), possibility (might/may), and advice (should/ought to). Each modal changes the strength of the meaning.
exercise: Write 8 sentences about rules, advice, or possibilities in your daily life using 4 different modal verbs.

content: Build arguments with a clear claim, supporting evidence, and a counterargument followed by a rebuttal. Use cohesive devices: furthermore, however, nevertheless, as a result.
exercise: Write a structured argument (12–15 sentences) for or against remote work. Include one counterpoint and a conclusion.

content: Use the passive when the agent is unknown, unimportant, or obvious. Form: be + past participle. Avoid overuse — prefer active when the agent matters.
exercise: Rewrite 5 active sentences as passive. Then write 3 original passive sentences on a topic of your choice.

content: Zero conditional (facts), first conditional (real future), second conditional (hypothetical present), third conditional (past hypothetical). Each uses a specific tense combination.
exercise: Write one sentence for each of the four conditional types. Then write a short paragraph (5–6 sentences) using at least two types naturally.

content: Use reference words (it, they, this, the former), lexical chains (synonyms, related words), and discourse markers (in contrast, consequently, to summarise) to create flow.
exercise: Write a paragraph of 8 sentences on climate change. Underline or label each cohesive device you use.

content: Use nominalisations (investigate→investigation), hedging language (it appears that, it could be argued), and impersonal constructions. Avoid contractions and informal vocabulary.
exercise: Rewrite 5 informal sentences in formal academic style. Then write a short abstract (80–100 words) for an imaginary study.

content: Express stance and nuance with adverbs (arguably, remarkably, inevitably), concession structures (while it is true that... it is also the case that...), and attitude markers.
exercise: Write a nuanced opinion piece (150 words) on a controversial topic. Use at least 3 stance adverbs and one concession structure.

content: Match vocabulary and syntax to audience: formal (Latinate vocabulary, complex sentences), neutral, or informal (Anglo-Saxon words, contractions, ellipsis). Avoid mixing registers.
exercise: Write the same message (asking for a deadline extension) in three registers: to a close friend, to a teacher, and to a senior manager.
